perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with and governed by the substantive internal laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted in a manner generally consistent with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'generally consistent' introduces ambiguity. New York law requires contracts to be construed in accordance with its specific laws, not just in a way that is generally aligned with them.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-1401 states that agreements containing a provision choosing New York law shall be governed by and construed under New York law. The modification weakens this requirement by only requiring that the agreement be 'generally consistent' with New York law, allowing for interpretations that may not be fully compliant with New York legal standards.

Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to New York's substantive internal laws, whereas the changed text only requires the contract to be 'generally consistent' with these laws. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to comply with New York law. NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-1401, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly states that agreements choosing New York law shall be governed by and construed under New York law. The change to 'generally consistent' allows for interpretations that might not fully adhere to New York legal standards, which directly contradicts the requirement of being governed by New York law.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice, instruction, direction or demand under the terms of this Agreement required to be in writing shall be duly given upon delivery, if delivered by hand, facsimile transmission, or mail, to the following addresses:
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice, instruction, direction or demand under the terms of this Agreement may be given in writing, potentially via hand delivery, facsimile transmission, or mail, to the following addresses:
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be duly given' to 'may be given' and 'required to be in writing shall be duly given' to 'may be given in writing' introduces ambiguity regarding whether written notice is mandatory. The insertion of 'potentially' before the methods of delivery introduces further ambiguity about whether those methods are acceptable or guaranteed to be effective. This contradicts the idea of 'due' notice, which requires a certain level of formality and reliability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 2103
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2103 specifies how papers are to be served in civil actions. While it doesn't explicitly mandate methods for contractual notices, it establishes a standard for proper service. The modified text introduces uncertainty about whether the specified methods are sufficient or required, potentially undermining the legal validity of notices served under the contract, since it goes against the established standard.

Service of papers. (a) Who can serve. Except where otherwise prescribed by law or order of court, papers may be served by any person not a party of the age of eighteen years or over. (b) Upon an attorney. Except where otherwise prescribed by law or order of court, papers to be served upon a party in a pending
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a specific procedure for providing notice, ensuring clarity and legal certainty. The changed text introduces ambiguity by using "may be given" instead of "shall be duly given," and adding "potentially" before the delivery methods. This undermines the requirement for 'due' notice, making it unclear whether written notice is mandatory and whether the specified delivery methods are guaranteed to be effective. This alteration directly affects the enforceability of the notice provision. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (NY CPLR § 2103) establishes a standard for proper service of papers. By introducing ambiguity, the modified text deviates from this standard, potentially undermining the legal validity of notices served under the contract.

perturbation.original_text: each of the parties hereby consents to the jurisdiction of such courts (and of the appropriate appellate courts therefrom) in any such suit, action or proceeding and irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of the venue of any such suit, action or proceeding in any such court or that any such suit, action or proceeding which is brought in any such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: each of the parties expresses a preference for the jurisdiction of such courts (and of the appropriate appellate courts therefrom) in any such suit, action or proceeding and waives, to the extent deemed reasonable, any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of the venue of any such suit, action or proceeding in any such court or that any such suit, action or proceeding which is brought in any such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'consents' with 'expresses a preference' weakens the agreement to jurisdiction, making it non-binding. Adding 'to the extent deemed reasonable' introduces subjectivity and allows a party to potentially challenge the venue despite the initial agreement. This conflicts with the established legal principles promoting forum selection clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3211(a)(4)
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 3211(a)(4) addresses motions to dismiss based on another action pending between the same parties. While not directly about forum selection clauses, it reflects New York's judicial efficiency concerns. By introducing ambiguity and weakening the jurisdictional agreement, the modified text could lead to increased litigation over venue, potentially undermining the purpose of pre-agreed forums and conflicting with the spirit of CPLR § 3211(a)(4) by creating opportunities for duplicative lawsuits.

Motion to dismiss. (a) Motion to dismiss cause of action. A party may move for judgment dismissing one or more causes of action asserted against him on the ground that: 1. a defense is founded upon documentary evidence; or 2. the court has not jurisdiction of the subject matter of the cause of action; or 3. the party asserting the cause of action has not legal capacity to sue; or 4. there is another action pending between the same parties for the same cause of action in a court of any state or the United States; the court need not dismiss upon this ground but may make such order as justice requires; or 5. the cause of action may not be maintained because of arbitration and award, collateral estoppel, discharge in bankruptcy, infancy or other disability of the moving party, payment, release, res judicata, statute of limitations, or statute of frauds; or 6. with respect to a counterclaim, it may not properly be interposed in the action; or 7. the pleading fails to state a cause of action; or 8. the court has not jurisdiction of the person of the defendant; or 9. the court has not jurisdiction in an action where service was made under section 314 or 315; or 10. the court should not proceed in the absence of a person who should be a party. 11. the party is immune from liability pursuant to section seven hundred twenty-a of the not-for-profit corporation law. Presumptive evidence of the status of the corporation, association, organization or trust under section 501 (c) (3) of the internal revenue code may consist of production of a letter from the United States internal revenue service reciting such determination on a preliminary or final basis or production of an official publication of the internal revenue service listing the corporation, association, organization or trust as an organization described in such section, and presumptive evidence of uncompensated status of the defendant ma
perturbation.justification: The original text expresses consent to jurisdiction and waives objections to venue to the fullest extent permitted by law. The changed text weakens this agreement by replacing 'consents' with 'expresses a preference' and adding 'to the extent deemed reasonable.' This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, potentially allowing a party to challenge venue despite the initial agreement. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this conflicts with legal principles promoting forum selection clauses. While the Law Explanation cites NY CPLR § 3211(a)(4), the relevance is weak. However, the modification creates an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, because the change makes the jurisdictional agreement non-binding, and allows a party to challenge venue, thus contradicting the purpose of a forum selection clause. This falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. The original text is a firm consent while the revised version is merely a preference.
